title: File-Delivery Outcomes, Boundaries, and Evidence · Guided Systems Studio
summary: A delivery team incrementally builds file-delivery outcome and evidence map, changes one file or failure, and keeps earlier HTTP, Go, accessibility, security, and resource-budget invariants active. Planned artifact: Build a complete first version of file-delivery outcome and evidence map while every earlier competency remains active. Learners progress through prediction, explicit theory, changed-case practice, causal debugging, and evidence defense.
objectives: Define file-delivery success with learner, uploader, viewer, owner, security, accessibility, operations, and finance evidence.
Trace browser, Go service, object store, CDN, identity provider, scanner, database, and stakeholder custody without assigning evidence a boundary cannot observe.
Separate business file identity, object key, object version, representation bytes, metadata, checksum, URL, and cache entry.
Preserve previously learned constraints and attach observable changed-case evidence.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Stakeholder Outcome
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 3996 during a regional dependency incident. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate stakeholder outcome on authorization, custody, accessibility, and cost evidence; keep browser execution deterministic and move AWS claims to an authorized transfer gate; then cancel halfway through and prove every body, part, and goroutine reaches a terminal state. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Define file-delivery success with learner, uploader, viewer, owner, security, accessibility, operations, and finance evidence.

What must be established before implementation?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Stakeholder Outcome.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for define file-delivery success with learner, uploader, viewer, owner, security, accessibility, operations, and finance evidence. For S3, CloudFront, and Go Commit to the mission model · Workshop Stakeholder Outcome, keep Stakeholder Outcome connected to executable Go storage and delivery decision evidence.

Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan
steps.instruction:
Append a compile-ready pure-Go function headed "// Evidence: storage-go-transfer-evidence-plan-jo3hkq" that uses case 5081 to gate stakeholder outcome on authorization, custody, accessibility, and cost evidence. Return observable changed-case evidence. Browser code must not import AWS SDK packages, open sockets, contact S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, networks, databases, or external effects, read host state, execute host commands, or use credentials; verify those boundaries later with Go 1.26.5 compile, vet, fuzz, race, leak, controlled AWS, load, fault, security, cost, restore, and production gates.

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan.
steps.why: Building a runnable or mechanically reviewable artifact turns Transfer Evidence Plan into observable performance evidence. content.caption: Shape of the required increment; replace generic language with the current stakeholder case.

content.title: Evidence is more than syntax
content.text: The structural check is only one gate. The following changed-case decision and explanation must also hold.

hints: For S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan, name the stakeholder, file, object version, authorization, byte, time, concurrency, cache, cost, cleanup, and recovery boundary, Transfer Evidence Plan precondition, or invariant that must hold.

At S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan, trace this concrete input and predict authorization, custody, integrity, range, cache, retry, cost, cleanup, and recovery evidence before reviewing it: A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 9567 during an SDK and infrastructure canary. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate stakeholder outcome on authorization, custody, accessibility, and cost evidence; do not treat an ETag, CORS result, emulator, or successful SDK call as stronger proof than it is; then double concurrent transfers and keep process resource limits intact. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled AWS tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence.

For S3, CloudFront, and Go Extend the cumulative artifact · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan, challenge the documented misconception with file, object, policy, transfer, integrity, cache, fault, cost, restore, and controlled AWS transfer evidence: A browser simulation or local emulator proves AWS storage, CDN, IAM, encryption, and recovery behavior.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan
steps.instruction:
workshop case — A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 4682 during a cost-anomaly investigation. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate stakeholder outcome on authorization, custody, accessibility, and cost evidence; retain stable tenant and file identity without exposing it in metric labels; then change the object version and prove stale authority is rejected. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled AWS tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence.

One precondition fails while output still looks plausible. What is the correct response?

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan.
steps.why: The decision supplies observable evidence for plan deterministic browser models, go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled aws tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence. For S3, CloudFront, and Go Test an acceptance boundary · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan, keep Transfer Evidence Plan connected to executable Go storage and delivery decision evidence.

content.title: Fault-isolation decision
content.text: Select evidence that identifies a cause, not a patch that only hides the Transfer Evidence Plan symptom.

content.label: Authority to consult after committing
content.value: HTTP Semantics RFC 9110 — Representations, methods, validators, conditional requests, ranges, partial content, and metadata.

stimulus.title: Transfer Evidence Plan failure evidence
stimulus.caption: A plausible visible result conflicts with the governing contract and requires a cause-level repair.

lines.label: observed
lines.text: A browser simulation or local emulator proves AWS storage, CDN, IAM, encryption, and recovery behavior.

lines.label: required
lines.text: A changed authentic task independently demonstrates this outcome: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled AWS tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence.

options.text: Reject the unsupported conclusion, name the violated Transfer Evidence Plan condition, repair the responsible layer, and rerun the boundary trace.
Keep the plausible output and hide the assumption to avoid confusing the stakeholder. That claim does not survive the test an acceptance boundary evidence boundary.
For test an acceptance boundary, run one convenient Transfer Evidence Plan happy path, omit ownership, error, changed input, goroutine stop, and full-toolchain checks, then accept plausible output.

steps.title: S3, CloudFront, and Go Defend the transferred model · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan
steps.instruction:
Write the workshop evidence note for Transfer Evidence Plan: explain the governing condition, one failure, and how this concrete case was verified — A workshop Go file-delivery team handles case 6301 during a compliance restore drill. Build deterministic Go evidence to gate stakeholder outcome on authorization, custody, accessibility, and cost evidence; retain stable tenant and file identity without exposing it in metric labels; then change cache and retrieval cost assumptions and defend the new budget result. Browser code uses pure values and state transitions only. AWS SDK packages, networks, S3, CloudFront, KMS, IAM, WAF, databases, credentials, host state, race and leak checks, load, faults, restore, billing, and production behavior require explicit authorized transfer gates. Competency probe: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled AWS tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence.

Evidence target: S3, CloudFront, and Go Defend the transferred model · Workshop Transfer Evidence Plan.

content.title: Evidence defense
content.text: Use the case, one assumption, one observable result, and one limitation. Do not copy the competency statement: Plan deterministic browser models, Go compile and race gates, disposable integration, controlled AWS tests, load, fault, restore, security, cost, and production evidence.
